data.title: Ahold Delhaize: While Tables Stay Empty, Ahold's Carts Stay Full
data.description:
Ahold Delhaize (OTCQX:ADRNY)(OTCQX:AHODF) has been one of my top defensive grocery picks, and so far it’s holding up nicely.

The gains aren't massive yet, but the story here is long-term compounding—steady dividends and consistent buybacks that help it quietly beat the market over time. So far, this stock has given nearly 2.7% in capital gains, and once you throw in dividends and buybacks, the total return is already past 4%—all in under a month.

It’s hard not to draw a comparison with another grocery name I cover here on Seeking Alpha: Weis Markets (WMK).

Since my first write-up, Weis has slipped almost 7%, a clear example of how sticking too rigidly to an ultra-conservative capital allocation strategy can weigh on market value.

But did Q2 really move the needle on our 'Buy' thesis?

After all, we were facing interesting developments such as:

  1. Deepening the value message through private labels, pricing investments (making them more competitive in a softer consumer environment), and an omnichannel strategy (developing loyalty platforms that resonate with guests). This applies to both its brands in the United States (Food Lion, Giant, Hannaford, Stop & Shop, etc.) and in Europe (Albert Heijn, Delhaize, Gall & Gall, etc.);
  2. Stop & Shop's turnaround, which is struggling and dragging down same-store sales in the United States, has closed 32 underperforming stores so far and is investing in recovery efforts;
  3. A ban on tobacco sales in the Benelux region for grocery stores, supermarkets, and other establishments. This proposal seeks to phase out tobacco sales entirely. By 2030, it would be banned from all outlets—including gas stations—restricted only to specialist tobacco shops starting in 2032, with a full prohibition targeted for 2040.

Of course, what I really want to gauge is whether Ahold Delhaize can keep acting like a cash machine—maintaining those steady annual payouts that, historically, have topped 6%. Sitting at $41, we're looking at nearly 25% upside when you factor in capital gains, buybacks, and dividends.

That’s exactly what income investors are after when they go shopping for defensive names.

Tailwinds for Grocers Continue to Blow

We're still looking at the same setup we talked about last time: eating at home keeps getting cheaper compared to dining out. And this accelerated in July.

In every month of Q2—except May, when both categories ticked up 0.3%—the cost of eating out rose faster than eating at home. That's why lower-income households keep holding off on restaurant visits, and when they do go, they're focused on stretching every dollar for maximum value.

The gap widened even further in July, with eating-at-home prices falling 0.1%, while eating out increased 0.3%. This was the largest gap since April!

Looking at an annualized basis, eating-at-home prices increased 2.2%, lagging behind headline inflation over the same period (2.7%) and far behind the 3.9% year-over-year price increase for eating out (a 1.7% gap over the last twelve months).

Add it all up, and you'll see a perfect environment for a healthy increase in traffic in grocery stores and supermarkets that can attract price-sensitive consumers. The ones that really get it—focusing on private labels and keeping prices sharp—are the ones pulling more people through the door.

How do I know this?

By compiling data from grocery space, we've been able to see some interesting things about the performance of grocery stores and Ahold Delhaize as a whole.

For every month of FY 2025 since February, Grocery Stores have outperformed all categories analyzed in year-over-year traffic growth by a wide margin.

Interestingly, in April—which saw the largest monthly gap between eating at home and eating out in recent years—Grocery Stores experienced the highest growth in the last two years (+4% year-over-year traffic growth).

During Q2, growth peaked in April, decelerating in the following months, reaching 1.1% year-over-year growth in June. By July, growth slowed even more to 0.7%, closing the gap with Superstores, FSRs, and QSRs.

So, overall, we continue to see tailwinds for Grocery Stores overall.

But what if we look closer at Ahold Delhaize?

We can break it down by Delaware, South Carolina, Virginia, and North Carolina (markets where Food Lion is a major player) and see whether they gained or lost market share over that period.

  • Delaware: Food Lion lost 0.7% of the market share, as it didn't grow like some of its competitors, but remains the largest grocery store in the state with a 24.1% share. Giant, on the other hand, gained 2.3% share, ending with a 10.8%.
  • South Carolina: Food Lion held onto its spot as the top chain in the state with a 30.4% market share, even picking up an extra 0.1%.
  • Virginia: Food Lion gained 0.5% market share in the period, finishing with 31.3% at the end of July.
  • North Carolina: Food Lion gained 0.5% of market share in the analyzed segment, ending with a market share of 41.9% in July.

All in all, Ahold Delhaize continues to hold the price advantage, is on the right side of the industry, and is gaining market share.

It's pretty safe to say that the Grocery Stores' tailwinds continue to blow.

This Cash Cow Still Has Many Options

Even with more than thirty underperforming Stop & Shop stores closed and the tobacco sales ban in the Benelux, Ahold Delhaize managed to pull in €23.1 billion in total revenue. This represents an increase of approximately 6.5% year-over-year.

Here's what drove this outcome:

  1. Same-store sales grew 3.4% year-over-year in the United States. This represents a sequential acceleration from the last quarter and a significant acceleration from the 0.4% decline in the same period last year. As restaurant inflation continues to rise, it's possible this will accelerate further in Q3;
  2. Same-store sales also accelerated in Europe by 4.9% year-over-year. This also represented a sequential acceleration, but perhaps most striking is that comparisons aren't as easy as in the United States;
  3. Investment in digital is paying off. Online sales reached €2.5 billion (10.8% of total sales), accelerating 14.4% year-over-year, faster than total sales, and generating incremental revenue.

Breaking this down, we can see that the United States continues to represent the largest chunk of quarterly revenue, with 57% of total sales (€13.2 billion, growing 1.9% year-over-year). Meanwhile, Europe represented 43% of total sales (€9.9 billion, growing 13.4% year-over-year thanks to strong performance in Benelux, which offset the tobacco ban).

The U.S. is still dealing with the impact of Stop & Shop's restructuring. According to some sources, traffic ticked up 3.1% month-over-month, but it's still down 5% year-over-year—something that continues to weigh on overall revenue growth.

Pricing investments to keep them more competitive also took a chunk out of margins in the United States. To be more precise, this had a 30 bp impact on the segment's underlying margin, which was 4.4% in the quarter.

Meanwhile, it remained stable in Europe at 3.7%.

All in all, Ahold Delhaize reported EBIT of €919 million (up 7.8% year-over-year), with an EBIT margin of 3.9%, about 10 bp higher year-over-year.

Due to higher net income and low single-digit fewer shares outstanding, they were able to report EPS of €0.60, a 13.2% year-over-year increase.

Everything seems very good here, despite the headwinds I mentioned. But do they continue to have strong cash flow?

The short answer is yes. Ahold reported OCF hit €1.59 billion, the third-highest level in the past two years. Subtract the €618 million in Capex, and you'll see FCF of €972 million.

That was a 17.4% year-over-year increase! Of course, we saw some benefits related to operating assets, but even if we adjust for OCF, the difference is clear, as both net income and D&A grew during this period.

As I've been pointing out, Ahold can comfortably generate the cash it needs to reward shareholders while paying down debt. Even after handing back €948 million through dividends (€611 million) and buybacks (€337 million), plus chipping away €548 million of net debt, they're still holding a hefty €3.93 billion in cash.

Just to update you, with this quarter's buybacks, they still have €558 million available for buybacks in FY 2025. Going forward, they can reduce that by 13.28 million (approximately a 1.5% reduction). This should total a return of 2.7% year-to-date.

Add this to a 3.2% yield, and you'll see a shareholder yield of approximately 6%, basically the same as we saw in FY 2024, which is quite solid to me.

Of course, we could see dividends growing again, even if it's not specifically spelled out in the guidance.

But can they do it? Yes, they can. And what's more, they can do it without even touching the cash they're sitting on.

Speaking in dollars now, in my last write-up I told you I expected FCF between $3.4 and $4.5 billion. Consider that they're currently targeting €1 billion in buybacks ($1.16 billion) and $1.17 billion in dividends (assuming an annual payout of $1.30 and shares outstanding at the end of FY 2025). Note that adding the two together, we get $2.33 billion, which is below the bottom end of FCF.

At this point, they could increase dividends, embark on another round of M&A (as they recently did with Profi), and even further deleverage their balance sheet.

The market estimates an annual dividend of $1.38 for all of FY 2025 (already pricing in an increase of over 6%) and $1.47 for FY 2026 (another 6.5% increase year-over-year). If you multiply this by the 889.6 million shares outstanding, I estimate for the end of FY 2025, you get a distribution of $1.22 and $1.3 billion.

Both are already covered by the current FCF. So, assuming they expand it a bit further (either due to the already observed tailwinds or Stop & Shop's recovery), I don't see any problems here.

Ahold Delhaize Is Still a ‘Buy’

Ahold Delhaize remains a 'Buy' for me for a few simple reasons:

  1. Ability to deliver a strong annual shareholder yield of approximately 6%, which outperforms bonds by a wide margin. This is supported by strong cash generation and exemplary liquidity;
  2. Because they generate strong cash flow, they can consolidate smaller players and thus continue growing;
  3. I am confident that Ahold Delhaize can continue to capitalize on the gap between grocery and restaurant prices and generate incremental sales and loyalty from it;
  4. It trades at very low multiples. EV/EBITDA is 6.5x, lower than peers like Weis Markets (7.1x) and Kroger (KR) (8.2x). P/CF is only 4.8x, which is further evidenced by the FCF yield between 6.1% and 8.1% (based on my FCF projections, which I've already explained here).

That said, it's pretty clear to me the so-called 'Fast Food Exodus' is far from over—especially with casual dining well-positioned to capture some of that traffic, while grocery stores continue to pull guests in through aggressive low-price strategies. Ahold Delhaize is definitely one of these companies.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Akamai: Navigating A Costly Transition With An Unattractive Risk/Reward Profile
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I give a Sell rating for Akamai Technologies (NASDAQ:AKAM). In my opinion, AKAM's near-term growth outlook is simply not exciting at all. Its pivot into cloud computing requires a big capital outlay, and the security segment is decelerating. The legacy delivery segment also remains a drag on the P&L. All in all, I see limited catalysts for a stock re-rating in the foreseeable future.

Company Description

AKAM is a global provider of cloud services with a massively distributed global network that comprises more than 4,300 points of presence across ~130 countries. The business is structured around three primary solution categories. The security portfolio, its largest segment, offers a suite of solutions to protect websites, applications, APIs, and infrastructure from cyberattacks. The Delivery segment consists of its legacy Content Delivery Network [CDN] services, which are designed to optimize the performance of media delivery and web applications. The cloud computing segment provides a range of infrastructure-as-a-service [IaaS] offerings, including compute, storage, and database services.

2Q25 Earnings Review

While AKAM 2Q25 headline numbers appeared strong, with revenue of $1.043 billion growing 7% y/y (beating the high end of guidance), the reality is that the business is deteriorating. For what it’s worth, the beat was primarily driven by the legacy delivery business, which saw its revenue decline moderated to -3% y/y (fell -13% in 2Q24). Although the security segment posted 11% y/y growth, which is great on a standalone basis, it essentially confirms that the deceleration trend has continued (security growth was 15% in 2Q24). As for the cloud computing segment, which was supposed to be AKAM’s future growth driver, its growth has also continued to decelerate, now just 13% y/y in 2Q24 vs. 14.5% in 2Q25 and >20% in FY24. As such, I am not feeling positive about the upgrade in guidance at all. Especially since management's decision to raise FY25 revenue guidance was partially due to re-including revenue from TikTok.

Security Growth Is Softening

The security segment, which was considered the main growth driver historically for AKAM, has apparently reached an inflection point where growth starts to plateau or even decelerate. In 2Q25, this segment only grew 10% y/y on a constant currency basis, which marked a significant deceleration from the high teens to 20% y/y growth rates it used to deliver. To me, this suggests that this segment is running into saturation and/or intensifying competition.

On the saturation front, the core products (Web Application Firewall [WAF] and DDoS protection) are not new products. These offerings have been in the market for many years, and naturally, penetration across AKAM's existing customer base is already very high. Most customers who needed WAF or DDoS solutions have purchased them, which leaves a much smaller pool of incremental demand, and consequently, growth slows.

On the competitive side of things, I believe the landscape has fundamentally changed. Large cybersecurity vendors (as mentioned by Palo Alto Networks in its 4Q25 earnings) are now pushing integrated platforms that bundle multiple security services into one offering. This often includes WAF and DDoS features by default. As a result, the value proposition of AKAM’s standalone solutions becomes less attractive and more commoditized, which means more pricing pressure as well.

Put together, these dynamics make it clear to me that the security segment is transitioning from a growth tailwind into a more mature, slower-growing business. Management’s strategy to combat this deceleration hinges on its newer products in API Security and Zero Trust from the Noname and Guardicore acquisitions. It is true that these products are growing rapidly, but their current revenue base is simply not large enough to move the needle.

Compute Ambitions Face a Costly Uphill Battle

As for the cloud computing segment, it is proving to be a costly, long-term bet with significant execution risk. For reference, the Compute segment accounts for only ~16% of total revenue, which is still relatively small compared to the other segments. Management is guiding for FY25 CapEx to be ~20% of revenue, as it needs to build out new capacity and "uplevel" the acquired Linode platform for enterprise clients. Despite this capex guide, management downgraded its FY25 compute growth to "a little less than" 15% y/y. Their reason was because of the uncertain timing of large deals, but I think this just adds another layer of uncertainty to the earnings outlook. This tells me that there will be periods of elevated capital outlay alongside growth deceleration, which I don’t expect the market to digest positively. In those periods, there will be a risk of consensus downgrading estimates to stay conservative, and that would limit multiple upside for the stock.

Valuation

I am not surprised that AKAM’s valuation (NTM PE) continues to see a downward trend (down from mid-to-high teens to just 11x today) because the fundamentals have indeed worsened. The legacy delivery segment is still in decline (though the decline has moderated, it doesn’t change the fact growth is still negative); the security segment growth is decelerating as it faces commoditization and competition risk; and the compute segment has a volatile growth outlook (and has a massive capital outlay required). As such, despite the stock screening cheap relative to history, I believe AKAM’s stock will be range-bound at best for the foreseeable future since, in the near term, there aren’t any strong catalysts to drive a turnaround.

Risk

The immediate upside risk is that AKAM investments in its Compute business may yield returns faster than I expected. If large enterprise deals, particularly those driven by new AI inference workloads, ramp up ahead of schedule, AKAM could see a meaningful acceleration in revenue growth. Furthermore, if there is faster-than-expected adoption of the newer API Security and Zero Trust solutions, it could reignite growth in the security segment.

Conclusion

I give a sell rating for AKAM. The main growth driver, security, is showing signs of maturity as its growth rate decelerates. Meanwhile, the pivot to cloud computing requires substantial ongoing investment but is not yet large enough to meaningfully move the needle. Finally, the legacy delivery business remains a structural drag. In my view, the risk/reward is not attractive, and it is difficult to justify a higher valuation at this point.

data.title: Tejon Ranch: Theoretically Cheap Compared To Land Value, But Uncertainties Remain
data.description:
Tejon Ranch Co. (NYSE:TRC) is a real estate development company with a significant portion of land in California, not too far from Los Angeles, where they intend to expand upon existing leases from existing commercial/industrial, mineral resources, farming and ranch operations with considerable residential/resort developments. While quite a lot of this land is still subject to full approvals from state government due to a legal row with some environmental groups, the upside potential could be significant in time and TRC is also a decent rate play, though we still need to see a couple more months of readings before we can say whether rate cuts are in the plans. The issue is realising upside tends to be very protracted and subject to lengthy regulatory approvals and then relatively lengthy building cycles, and the rate environment certainly makes it harder for developers. It's worth watching, but we aren't buying at the moment. Though we do want to mention that it seems an LBO firm that works in partnership with management in the middle market, TowerView Capital, has a considerable stake. There have also been some activist preludes, met with the typical responses. In this case, the government is such a major stakeholder that we question how much shareholder change will matter.

Breakdown

Some existing leases are on the TRCC and are benefiting the Interstate 5 significantly, which is a very traveled road in the USA. They even have a free trade zone in there as well. They also do some activity through JVs.

Within our commercial/industrial segment, we lease land to various types of tenants. We currently lease land to two auto service stations with convenience stores, 12 fast-food operations, one service diner-style restaurant, a motel, an antique shop, and a post office.

10-K 2024

The progress was flat in the overall commercial/industrial leases when ignoring the effects of a property sale in the segment. Outside of the commercial/industrial revenues comes income from mineral resources, farming, and ranch operations.

The Q2 mineral resources sales come primarily from cement, oil and gas, and rock aggregates. Usually, there is a contribution from water sales as well. Apparently, strong rainfall seasons in California prevented the ability to sell water from the property. Lower production volumes also hit cement sales, owing to a poorer construction environment.

For farming, the growth was from an almond carryover crop sold in the period relative to a very easy comp in the previous year. But there wasn't a meaningful pickup in profit. In the ranch operations, there was more guided hunt activity.

Other than the mineral resources and TRCC commercial operations, none of the businesses were running profitably for the 3 months.

The forward view concerns the residential/resort segment. The idea, in particular, for the Mountain Village segment is that there could be demand for living facilities based on the commercial activities happening on Tejon land. Centennial is another important project that, unfortunately, saw as a consequence of litigation the need to get further approvals, particularly on wildfire risk mitigation. The approvals in other words have been rescinded and the process continues. Nonetheless, the residential developments are likely where the upside would be here and they mostly own the entities that are conducting these developments, although the financing solution is still outstanding for Mountain Village, which is the one that's been approved already as of 2021.

We aren't sure what it's worth exactly and the extent of value creation, but crudely using Redfin data from the zip codes around Tejon, we get that the land value might be around $820 million overall.

This is a very crude valuation. Moreover, we don't expect the price of the stock to reflect this valuation. In addition to the approximately $76 million in net debt, Centennial is still a question mark with approvals and the timeline of making much out of these assets seems protracted, as the company is still out looking for financing options to our understanding for the already approved Mountain Village. The stock trades at around 1.6x that value.

Bottom Line

We have looked at companies a little bit like this before, taking a back-of-the-envelope approach. It looks about in line with the others in terms of valuation when including the existing lease streams and activities. It gets closer to the 2x mark when applying a 5% cap rate on the industrial/commercial leases and assuming some baseline value for the water rights and other assets they have. It could be interesting to see the emerging dynamics with rates. While we are still waiting for the Jackson Hole speeches, what we are paying attention to is simply that the labour market copped some nasty downward revisions, they tend to be procyclical, and there are other labour market indicators that are cause for some degree of concern. Of course, inflation remains above target, and hawkishness has been the way for some time now. But incrementally, we feel the growth mandate and rate cuts that may help companies like TRC acquire financing may be coming soon.

data.title: Monster Beverage: Near-Term Uncertainties Temper My Conviction For A Buy
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Monster Beverage (NASDAQ:MNST) in June, which I downgraded to a hold rating as valuation has caught up with fundamentals, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. MNST's 2Q25 quarter was very strong, with record sales crossing the $2 billion mark for the first time and margins expanding meaningfully. However, at the same time, my concerns around US share pressure and potential cost headwinds remain. Taken together, I think the story is balanced—strong fundamentals and pricing power offset by competitive risks and near-term uncertainties—and that is enough to keep me neutral at the current levels.

The 2Q25 results

My view is that MNST delivered an excellent 2Q25, with net sales up 11.1% y/y to $2.11 billion, marking the first time the company crossed the $2 billion quarterly mark. On a constant currency basis, growth was even stronger at 11.4% y/y. The volume story is also impressive, in that energy drink case sales grew 17.5% y/y, reflecting healthy demand across markets. By segments, the core Monster Energy Drinks business remains the core driver, growing 11.2% y/y to $1.94 billion in sales. Strategic Brands did well too, growing by 18.9% y/y to $129.9 million, showing that diversification efforts are contributing. The main soft spot was alcohol brands, which declined 8.6% y/y to $38 million. Profitability was just as strong as the top line. Gross profit grew 15.4% y/y, with margin expanding 210 bps to 55.7%. Combined with cost control, this drove EBIT up 19.8% y/y to $631.6 million. Ultimately, diluted EPS grew 21.1% to $0.50.

Fundamental Outlook Remains Strong

I believe the fundamental drivers for MNST have improved, especially on the profitability front. The 2Q25 gross margin expansion was impressive, one that I think reflects a combination of things that MNST has executed really well: late-2024 pricing actions, supply chain efficiencies, and reduction in input costs. This development does push back on my concern that MNST margin will see pressure as it steps up promotion (refer to my previous post where I showed a survey done by Goldman Sachs). Turns out, if MNST can structurally improve gross margin, it actually can continue to be aggressive on promotions to win market share (if it wants to).

And yes, I do see a visible path for MNST to continue improving gross margin structurally, as management has already signaled plans for another round of selective price increases and reduced promotional allowances in the US, set to take effect in 4Q25. In my opinion, this clearly shows that MNST has strong pricing power, and given that there is still plenty of headroom to raise prices, this should continue to be a topline growth and gross-margin expansion driver. We know there is room to raise prices because in the call, management noted that energy drink prices have seen a smaller magnitude of price increase vs. other non-alcoholic ready-to-drink [NARTD] beverages over the past decade. With consumers now showing positive preference for energy drinks (i.e., the value proposition is resonating), I expect consumers to be more open to accepting price increases.

Taking a step back, the overall demand backdrop also remains very healthy. Energy drink consumption globally is not just holding up; it is actually accelerating, with scanner data showing double-digit growth across all key regions: 13.2% y/y in the U.S., 15.4% in EMEA, and an impressive 20.9% in Asia Pacific (data from the 2Q25 call). This, to me, is showing very clearly that the category is still in a growth phase. If you look at the growth figures, you would see that overseas growth is better, and I think this is going to be the next leg of MNST’s growth story. For MNST, there is a similar growth trend, in that net sales outside the US grew 15.8% y/y in 2Q25 and now account for 41% of total revenue, up from 39% a year ago. This shows that MNST is successfully tapping into international markets.

Putting together MNST’s pricing power, reduced promotional activities, international momentum, and operating leverage, I would not be surprised that earnings continue to compound at a robust rate, backed by top-line growth and margin expansion.

Competitive Pressures, Cost Headwinds, and Valuation Remain

While 2Q25 execution was evidently strong, the concerns I discussed in my previous write-up regarding the US competitive landscape have not disappeared. I am still concerned about market share pressure. Recent scanner data for the 4-week period ending late July showed Monster’s dollar share in the US convenience and gas channel down 60 bps. To be clear here, the overall category strength is driving up MNST absolute sales, but this ongoing share loss is still a red flag to me, as it suggests a competitive backdrop. With management preparing another round of selective price increases in 4Q25, the risk is that if competitors choose not to follow, MNST could see further unit share pressure.

On the margin front, as I said above, 2Q25’s 210 bps expansion was great, but investors should also keep in mind the potential near-term headwinds. Management noted they expect a modest impact in 3Q25 from tariffs tied to the Midwest aluminum premium. While MNST has implemented hedging and pricing actions, which should help offset the effect, this does add a layer of uncertainty into the near-term margin outlook. And this brings me to my next point—MNST’s valuation.

Based on Bloomberg’s data, MNST trades at 31x forward PE, and while this is the past 5 years’ average and may look cheap, I don’t really think so. MNST’s valuation tracks closely to earnings growth expectation. The mid-to-long-term fundamentals are healthy, as I noted, which should continue to support this low-teens EPS growth level, but in the near term, with the unknown tariff impacts and pricing affecting market share, my fear is that near-term earnings may disappoint, and that would weigh on multiples. In the bull case, all my concerns are for nothing, and MNST goes on to accelerate EPS growth, driven by all the factors mentioned above, and that would drive multiples up to the high 30s potentially. But for now, I would just wait for another 1 or 2 quarters to confirm MNST is not losing more share and the pricing initiatives work out well.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for MNST remains a hold. The business is executing extremely well, with structural margin improvements, clear pricing power, and international momentum. However, my concerns regarding share loss in the US, potential cost pressures, and valuation at ~31x forward PE hold me back from upgrading my rating in the near term. I think another quarter or two of data will be critical to confirm that MNST is not losing more share and pricing actions flow through as intended. Until then, I prefer to stay patient.

data.title: ATRenew Reports Brilliant Q2 2025 Earnings Results
data.description:
ATRenew Inc. (NYSE:RERE) has just reported its Q2 2025 earnings. The company has reported its fourth consecutive quarter of positive GAAP net profit and brilliant sales growth. The business outlook announced by the management during the conference call was also highly positive. Let me give you some more details about the company’s earnings and also explain the recent stock price growth.

My previous article

In my previous article, I wrote about ATRenew’s strong Q1 2025 earnings results with 27.5% sales growth and sustainably increasing profit figures.

Also, I wrote about the Chinese government’s support of recycling, ATRenew’s robust retail network, and the company’s leadership in China’s circular economy.

I also wrote that ATRenew’s domestic focus and AI innovation present plenty of opportunities.

Despite its strong fundamentals and share buybacks, ATRenew’s stock was undervalued, presenting a great entry point for growth investors.

I was right, it seems, because since 22 May 2025, the date of my previous article’s publication, the stock has gained more than 73%. I will later explain why.

Q2 2025 earnings

Overall, we can safely say that ATRenew’s results have improved on all fronts.

ATRenew’s sales revenues increased by 32.2%, totaling RMB 4,991.5 million, or USD $696.8 million, compared to RMB 3,776.7 million for the same period a year ago. This was mostly due to the rise in the number of consumer products transacted. It totaled 10.3 million versus the 8.4 million for the same period a year ago.

Also, income from operations totaled RMB 91.1 million, or USD $12.7 million, versus an operating loss of RMB 5.6 million for the same period a year ago. Non-GAAP adjusted income from operations was RMB 121.3 million, or USD $16.9 million, versus the adjusted income from operations of RMB 94.1 million for the same period a year ago.

The table below, borrowed from ATRenew’s presentation, summarizes how much the company’s key earnings figures have improved. Apart from the positive changes that happened in Q2 2025, the first half of 2025 was also associated with improvements. There was almost a 30% increase in the total net revenues, in GAAP income from operations, and also in non-GAAP income from operations.

In addition, ATRenew also actively buys back its shares. During 2024 and the first half of 2025, the company bought back shares worth over $30 million, thus supporting the stock price.

The company’s scale of operation is growing fast as well. Its total store count totaled 2,092, a surge of 576 just year-on-year.

Obviously, such a brilliant sales improvement suggests strong demand growth for secondhand electronics and luxury goods in China.

As concerns ATRenew’s business outlook for Q3 2025, the company expects its sales to total between RMB 5,050 million and RMB 5,150 million, constituting a rise between 24.7% and 27.1% year-over-year.

ATRenew’s earnings history

As can be seen from the table below, the company’s quarterly earnings have been improving for a while.

ATRenew’s quarterly sales and income history

The data are given in millions of RMB, rounded to the nearest million

Especially the sales are in an obvious upside trend.

Also, the non-GAAP net income has started rising after Q3 2022.

Even though the GAAP net income trend is not so apparent from the diagram below, ATRenew has just reported the fourth consecutive quarter of positive GAAP net income. The recently reported figure of about RMB 72 million is also higher than the RMB 43 million reported for Q1 2025.

But how about the company’s financial fundamentals and RERE stock valuations?

ATRenew’s fundamentals and valuations

ATRenew’s balance sheet (the data are given in USDs)

Particularly important to me is the company’s strong balance sheet. As can be seen from the table above, the net debt (debt-cash) is negative, which suggests the company’s very low debt load and a huge cash pile. Also, the debt-to-equity ratio above suggests the company has low liabilities versus its assets. The current and covered ratios also suggest low and easily manageable debt. So, overall, the company’s balance sheet is very strong.

Let us also have a look at the company’s valuations.

From the two diagrams below, we can clearly see that despite RERE stock’s recent rally, it is still trading substantially lower compared to its post-IPO price of more than $15 per share, suggesting upside potential.

Moreover, if we see the valuation table below, all of the company’s valuation ratios, apart from the GAAP P/E, are lower than its peers’. However, ATRenew has been increasing its profitability. So, even its P/E GAAP (TTM) is highly likely to decrease in the future. Its forward P/E GAAP is just over 18.

Positive factors

According to ATRenew’s management’s conference call, the circular economy model that includes second-hand consumption has excellent growth potential thanks to younger generations’ interest in high quality for less money as well as their growing appreciation of environmentally friendly second-hand consumption.

Moreover, according to the management, in September this year, leading high-tech brands would launch new devices, which would provide ATRenew’s customers with significant upgrade opportunities. In other words, they would be able to exchange their old electronics for new models, thus generating extra revenues and profits for ATRenew.

ATRenew’s Q2 2025 results show that smartphones are their strongest growth factor. Thanks to the fact that trade-ins have become popular, the trade-in program launched with JD.com’s (JD) support saw strong growth.

In addition, the Chinese government’s subsidies signal their strong support for trade-ins. Furthermore, the Chinese government will probably pay a national subsidy in October, which will continue increasing ATRenew’s revenue and profit.

ATRenew’s management remains highly optimistic and plans to expand recycling and trade-in scenarios while strengthening store and door-to-door fulfillment.

Moreover, the management is confident about ATRenew’s revenue growth targets for the second half of 2025. The profitability outlook also remains strong. Thanks to the growing operating scale, there will also be substantial operating profit margin improvements next year that will create long-term value for ATRenew’s stockholders.

Downside risks

As concerns ATRenew’s downside risks, these are straightforward but quite irrelevant to growth-oriented investors. Some investors might prefer companies with long positive net GAAP profits. However, ATRenew has reported its fourth consecutive quarter of positive GAAP net income. And the management’s outlook suggests that the matters would only get better for ATRenew.

Tariffs may exert some pressure on the global economy, but they do not have a material impact on ATRenew’s actual operations. This is because ATRenew mostly operates in China and has little to do with U.S. consumers.

Conclusion

Overall, ATRenew has reported a great set of results, including growing sales and a fourth consecutive quarter of net profitability. The company’s stock is still cheaply valued despite the recent rally. The management remains highly optimistic about the future. Moreover, the company’s balance sheet and its cash position are strong, which is a great positive for investors.

data.title: Aegon Stock: 37% Rally Exceeds Targets, P/B Valuation Keeps Buy Rating Alive
data.description:
Aegon stock (NYSE:AEG) surged following the release of its H1 results exceeding my base case price target of $6.86 and my more optimistic price target of $7.78. The stock price appreciation of 37% significantly outpaces the S&P 500’s 5.3% gain. In this report, I briefly discuss the most recent earnings and update my price target for the stock still providing support for a buy rating.

If you are interested in understanding Aegon’s activities, I have provided the description from my prior report below:

What Does Aegon Do?

Aegon provides life insurance, retirement solutions and asset management services. In some way, you can view the company as a company that aids with financial planning and wealth management. The company is active in the U.S., U.K., Spain, Portugal, China, and Brazil.

Opportunities for Aegon include a growing middle class in some of its end markets such as China and Brazil, which could increase demand for life insurance solutions and asset management. There are also several risks for the company. One of those risks is the interest rate environment. Aegon, like many insurance companies and pension funds, relies on investment in fixed-income assets such as bonds. If the interest rate drops, the yield on newly issued bonds is lower and, as a result, the total yield on Aegon’s portfolio also comes down. At the same time, higher interest rates can also reduce demand for loans and credit life sales. Regulatory changes are also a risk to the business. In the third quarter of 2024, new life sales in China ticked up ahead of regulatory pricing changes. So, we see that a price change can provide a one-time bump in sales.

Another risk for the business is the simple fact that if demand shifts, for instance to higher yield products, Aegon might not be able to satisfy the needs of its clients or potential clients.

Stellar Performance For Aegon

Aegon posted stellar earnings with an increase of 19% in operating results and free cash flow surging 18%. Life insurance sales increased 13% in the Americas while net deposits increase from $1.2 billion to $1.7 billion for Retirement & Investments. In the UK, the company booked strong workplace pension platform inflows while international new life sales grew 3%. Asset management inflow fell from $7.8 billion to $4.4 billion driven by a pivot to fixed income products this year while last year it saw significant inflow of funds due to large contract wins. I am not too concerned about that since asset management typically has lumpy inflows.

The improvement in the operating results was primarily carried by the Americas were results improved €125 million, smaller contributions came from the UK, International and Asset Management with contributions of €14 million, €12 million and €3 million respectively partially offset by holding costs.

Aegon Seemingly Lacks Attractive Upside

For me, Aegon lacks attractive upside. Using a P/B valuation method, we see little upside. The P/B ratio of 1.35x matches that of the peer group and this currently shows Aegon is overvalued by around 10%. After we implement remaining cash inflows this year expected and dividend payments, the downside reduces to 9% and 10% after share repurchases are processed.

I also attempted to value the stock according to an actual calculated fair P/B-ratio. This ratio is calculated by subtracting a reasonable growth rate of 2% from the Return on Equity and from the Cost of Equity. The expected Return on Equity for 2025 is around 13.6%. Using a risk-free rate in line with the 10-Year Treasury Yield, a Beta of 1.25 and a Equity Risk Premium of 5% we get to a P/B ratio of 1.35x, which matches the peer group.

The Beta of 1.25x is used given the perceived risk associated with the insurance business. However, if we use a more nuanced Beta that actually reflects the stock price Beta, we get to a P/B ratio of 1.65x. This would indicate roughly 10-11 percent upside, which is good enough for me to maintain my buy rating for the stock as I also noted the current dividend yield of around 5%.

Conclusion: Aegon Has Upside

Aegon’s valuation, in essence depends on which P/B ratio one applies. A 1.35x ratio provided a downside scenario, but a fair P/B ratio keeping in mind return on equity and cost of equity provides a more compelling investment case. I am looking for stocks with double digit upside and there is indeed a scenario in which Aegon can deliver that. That is why I am maintaining a buy rating on the name but I do note that the investment case is less robust compared to my prior report on Aegon.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Live Nation Entertainment: Remain Bullish On This Stock
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Live Nation Entertainment (NYSE:LYV) was a buy rating because the underlying demand health improved, and forward-leading indicators of growth were also robust. I stay buy-rated for LYV as concert growth continues to accelerate, deferred revenue has hit record highs, and Venue Nation, international expansion, and sponsorship are all moving in the right direction. These growth drivers should continue to drive top-line growth and margin expansion.

2Q25 results update

LYV 2Q25 results did a lot better than I expected. For those that missed the earnings, total revenue was $7 billion, up 16% y/y, which is almost entirely from the core Concerts business. Concert revenue grew 19% y/y to ~$6 billion, driven by 14% y/y growth in the number of fans to 44 million. At first glance, Ticketing and Sponsorship looked more muted, with adj. operating income [AOI] down 1% y/y and up 2% y/y, respectively. But this is not on a like-for-like basis. Management made it clear they were affected by temporary factors like a single festival cancellation and geographic ticketing mix. In any case, I don’t think that has created a dent in LYV’s growth story. Looking down the P&L statement, total AOI grew 11% y/y to $798 million, which is expectedly led by the Concerts segment that delivered a record $359 million AOI in the quarter, up 33% y/y.

Consumer Demand Defies Macro Concerns

For investors worried about the macro drag on discretionary spending, I think LYV 2Q25 results should have convinced you that it is not being impacted by that headwind. Management explicitly noted that they see no change in consumer demand, and the data clearly supports this. Ticket sales remain robust across all tiers, with both premium seating and lower-cost options performing well, showing that consumers are still willing to pay up for premium experiences. The pace of advance sales is particularly telling. Specifically, tickets sold for 2025 events have already surged past 130 million, up sharply from 95 million just last quarter, and this strength shows up in deferred revenue, which increased 25% y/y to $5.1 billion for concerts and 22% y/y to $317 million for Ticketmaster. Note that this represents cash already collected, which essentially means LYV has locked in a large portion of future revenue.

Fans are spending more once they’re at events. Concession sales at large amphitheaters grew double-digits in 2Q25, and the recent Lollapalooza festival delivered record per-fan spending growth. These high-margin ancillary revenues suggest that engagement is not only strong at the point of purchase but also deepening at the venue.

Taken together, I believe the combination of accelerating ticket sales, record deferred revenue, and higher onsite monetization are very strong proof that consumer appetite for live experiences remains resilient.

Venue Nation Strategy Translates into Tangible Growth

The Venue strategy I highlighted is starting to show its worth in the LYV growth flywheel. To reiterate, my view was that by expanding LYV's portfolio of owned and operated venues, it is not only increasing capacity but also enhancing its monetization capabilities.

Venue ownership is very important because it allows LYV to control more of the economics across its ecosystem. Instead of relying on third-party operators, LYV is able to capture revenue from not just ticketing but also concessions, VIP seating, and sponsorships. RedFox Capital

In fact, management now expects to host ~70 million fans at its own venues this year, which is a double-digit increase, one that far outpaces the overall fan growth. This tells me that LYV is finding success in bringing in events to its own venues, and fans are indifferent between LYV’s or non-LYV’s venues (in terms of attendance).

Looking ahead, this strategy should continue to be a driver to growth as the development pipeline also looks robust. Based on the data disclosed, there are new venues opening in Mexico, Colombia, and Canada in 2H25, and another ten large venues set for 2026. These additions will add capacity for ~6 million fans on a run-rate basis, for perspective. Importantly, the economics are highly attractive. For reference, new venues are consistently generating >20% returns, while amphitheaters are delivering double-digit higher food and beverage sales compared to the legacy portfolio. As I said before, these incremental revenue streams carry very high margins, so the impact to earnings will be asymmetrical vs. the top line.

International Growth and Sponsorship

Lastly, the two other growth drivers—International and Sponsorship—have become increasingly important in driving growth.

For the international markets, performance was outstanding. Total global fan count grew 14%, and international attendance grew 30% y/y, which basically accounted for the entire 2Q25 growth. The demand momentum appears to continue, as management has guided for double-digit international fan growth through 2H25. The significance here goes beyond just more fans and attendance, but that Ticketmaster network becomes even larger. So far, Ticketmaster has signed 17 million net new enterprise tickets this year (~70% of which came from outside North America). This proved my previous point that this integrated model of promoting shows, operating venues, and selling tickets is creating a virtuous cycle in new markets. As international business scales,

That shows the global flywheel is kicking in. The more shows LYV put on internationally, the more fans they reached, and the more venues and markets they gained traction in. And because Ticketmaster is the platform behind those events, it gives LYV more control over the full fan journey—starting from when someone buys a ticket, all the way to how they’re marketed to, what they spend on at the venue, and how they come back for future events. Redfox Capital

At the same time, sponsorship continues to provide LYV with a baseline high-margin revenue stream. The 2% AOI growth in 2Q25 might look weak, but I remind readers that this was largely due to the one-off cancellation of the Bonnaroo festival. Stripping out that impact, the underlying sponsorship business is strong. In fact, visibility has improved a lot since my last update. Previously, I highlighted that 85% of sponsorship revenue was already contracted for the year. Today, that figure has increased to 95% of expected 2025 revenue, with committed sales pacing up double-digits y/y.

Valuation

Using the same valuation method I did previously, focusing on the mid-term EBITDA (FY27) using consensus estimates as an anchor (since they have been accurate), I see 16% upside for now. I think it makes sense that Consensus upgraded their FY27 EBITDA estimates given the consumer demand strength, and with this strong performance, I also expect the market to continue attaching a premium to LYV’s stock. The current forward EBITDA multiple of 16x is at the high end over the past 3 years, which I think is fair given the improvements in fundamentals. So, attaching 16x would translate to a share price target of $188, or 16% upside.

Risk

A prolonged economic downturn (or a recession) could significantly impact ticket sales for concerts and other events as consumers ultimately pull back from discretionary spending. There is also FX risk. Given that a significant portion of LYV revenue is generated internationally, a stronger US dollar would negatively impact reported results. Another risk worth flagging is the LYV core promotion model. It often provides artists with substantial financial guarantees (months of payout before a show actually takes place). While this approach is important to secure top talent, it carries risk. If demand weakens sharply in the lead-up to an event or if a show underperforms expectations, LYV still shoulders those upfront commitments.

Conclusion

My view for LYV is a buy rating. LYV 2Q25 results clearly show strong consumer appetite, record levels of forward sales, and solid execution in its venue strategy, sponsorship segment, and international expansion. The fundamentals are trending strongly in LYV’s favor, which I think justifies consensus earnings being revised higher and the stock deserving a premium multiple.

data.title: Why Nvidia's Comparison With Railroads Is Ominous
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation's (NASDAQ:NVDA) stock price has been on a tear since the tariff scare in Apr-25, almost doubling from early April lows of $90 to $180 per share.

This article will discuss recent developments that still supports a pessimistic case that NVDA is nowhere near worth a $4 trillion market cap in the long run. I will start with a recent research report that compared NVDA to railroads.

#1 Comparing NVDA with railroads: why this is more ominous than bullish

A recent equity research report from Bank of America suggested that railroads as a percent of U.S. stock market cap hit 63% in 1881 and maintained a relatively high level for decades.

Some bulls have compared this to the Magnificent Seven’s percentage of total U.S. S&P 500 (SP500) market cap (currently at around 34% in July 2025 as shown below) to prove that the M7’s proportion is actually not that high.

But my view is that only an investor that has experienced bull markets and limited if any experience of bear markets would hold such an opinion. The full picture below shows that railroads went from 63% of U.S. stock market weight in 1881 to near 0% by 2025. This is far from encouraging: it suggests that sectors that drive productivity may not fully capture the economic gains from such productivity and may even dwindle in their importance over the long run.

This is similar to a comparison with railroads, which incidentally I made nearly a year ago in October 2024 in this previous article. I argued that railroads were a cautionary tale for NVDA rather than a bullish comparison as:

  • railroads were a good example of a hot industry that was iconic in its era, similar to AI and NVDA in the present day. Yet, despite their popularity with investors in the 1800s, railroads delivered largely flat returns to shareholders from 1900 to 1970.

  • NVDA is also more akin to railroad construction (i.e., providing chips for data centers) rather than railroad operation (i.e., tech giants such as Microsoft (MSFT) or Meta (META) that directly face end users). Railroad construction is a “one-time“ thing: new track laid in terms of mileage peaked in 1887 and fell to nearly 0 by the early 1900s.

So if we look at the history of railroads, there are many sobering comparisons with AI and NVDA that are far more bearish than bullish. In fact, hoping that tech stocks could account for a higher percentage of total stock market cap without considering the downside risk (i.e., in the long run, these stocks could become far more diminished compared to where they are now) may be dangerous for individual investor. It’s akin to what Warren Buffett would call a cigarette butt play, where an investor picks up a cigarette butt to get one more puff out of it. But is one more puff worth it? The history of railroads suggests a resounding no.

The fact that so many investors are desensitized to risk and don’t even factor in the possibility that high flyers like NVDA could suffer a permanent loss to value suggests to me a market environment that is over-exuberant and prone to massive drawdowns. After all, if the key to earning large returns was as easy as ploughing money into highflying market darlings, investors practicing that strategy in 1999 or 2007 would have became rich long-time ago.

#2 Doubt is creeping into the AI story

AI has become a buzz word justifying rosy forecasts of growth and stock prices for the past few years. I’m a strong believer in AI’s potential and use AI tools during work and daily life and find them useful. However with these valuations, even AI becoming “mundane” could be negative for the valuations. There are emerging signs that the AI story will become less prominent and rosy, just to give a few examples:

  • OpenAI’s Sam Altman recently warned of an AI bubble, similar to dotcom bubble, reportedly stating that: “When bubbles happen, smart people get overexcited about a kernel of truth…Are we in a phase where investors as a whole are overexcited about AI? My opinion is yes. Is AI the most important thing to happen in a very long time? My opinion is also yes.” When the CEO of OpenAI is talking about a bubble (when it’s against his interests to talk about that) maybe it’s worth giving it some thought.

  • An MIT report that claimed 95% of companies investing in generative AI are seeing no returns. That actually fits my day-to-day observations, there is a lot of excitement from the corporate sector about generative AI, but from what I’ve seen, the actual deployment of AI (mainly some form of generative AI) in many companies may not meet lofty expectations. Sure, maybe a few junior positions can be eliminated, but that’s not a reason to be overly excited. That’s like saying you need a lot fewer junior level employees after computers were invented, but if everyone does it, there’s no competitive edge gained.

  • META freezing AI hiring (while it was offering large sign-up bonuses just last month)

NVDA has a market cap of $4.3 trillion and a TTM P/E of 55, as I discussed in my article last month, I think it’s unlikely NVDA will sell anywhere near the amount the chips to justify the $4 trillion market cap. NVDA’s market cap could suffer even if the market recalibrates its forecast for AI to a lower level than is currently embedded in the $4 trillion.

#3 Chip sales to China may not materialize

While NVDA regained permission to sell chips to China, recent news suggests China is reportedly wary of buying chips from NVDA and NVDA was reported today to halt H20 production. As I previously argued, NVDA resuming chip sales to China was a bearish rather than bullish sign and now those sales might not even materialize, which will further dent NVDA’s near term financials.

Risks to bearish thesis:

NVDA has been able to keep the bullish momentum so far, surpassing one market cap threshold after another ($1 trillion, $2 trillion ,$ 3 trillion and now $4 trillion) so it is plausible that the AI story could regain momentum yet again and keep plowing forward.

Conclusion:

In my view, dark clouds continue to gather that are bearish for NVDA’s long-term prospects. Again, I am not shorting nor do I suggest shorting NVDA as the tech bull market is too strong. However, individual investor may seriously reconsider if they have a large amount of their nest egg invested in NVDA. Even if AI truly delivers on its promises, other major tech giants are just as likely to benefit, there’s no reason to put all the AI eggs in one basket.

data.title: Joanna Gallegos On XHLF: A Smarter Solution For Duration Risk
data.description:
Follow XHLF on Seeking Alpha!

Daniel Snyder: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Content is offered for information purposes only. Unless stated otherwise, any and all individuals participating in the video are third parties that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body. Unless stated otherwise, the views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. The accuracy and completeness of content shared cannot be guaranteed. Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, US Investment adviser, or investment bank.

Hey, everyone. Daniel Snyder back with another ETF Spotlight. This week we're jumping into the world of Treasuries and Treasury Duration with none other than Joanna Gallegos, Co-Founder over at BondBloxx, and we're going to talk about the XHLF Fund today. So let's go ahead and dive into the conversation. Joanna, I just want to - give us a background of how this fund created and even the suite that you've built over there. Where did this come from? When did you see the need? Were people talking to you and they were like, we need this duration of treasury funds and you provided. How did this begin?

Joanna Gallegos: So we recognize that there was a problem in the current ETFs out there in the treasury space. And that core problem was duration drift. It's a significant issue that I think investors probably overlooked until interest rates started changing so dramatically about four years ago. US treasury ETFs are a popular and efficient way to gain exposure to treasuries for income generation and portfolio stability. But most of these ETFs, the current ETFs before our target duration ETFs came out, they hold bonds across a broad range of maturities, and that creates challenges for investors who need to keep their portfolios aligned to a specific duration target.

So what is duration drift and what is duration? Duration measures a bond sensitivity interest rate changes. And usually, you don't see such dramatic changes intra-year or over shorter periods of time. So a higher duration means greater price sensitivity. The bond's price is going to move if there's more duration in it faster. Managing duration is critical for optimizing returns, monitoring interest rate risk, and ensuring portfolios remain aligned with investment objectives and your risk tolerance.

DS: So specifically for types of investors that are maybe wanting to do treasuries without having to go through the Treasury Direct website and kind of do all that themselves, right? Is that kind of what these funds help alleviate that pain?

JG: Yeah. So, in traditional maturity-based treasury ETFs, duration is going to change over time as the bonds inside that portfolio age and market interest rates change or move. And this shifting sensitivity, which we described as duration risk, it can be really significant. I mean, in 2022 alone, we saw interest rates change 450 basis points. That was pretty shocking. And if you hadn't been paying attention to where your treasury duration was, your portfolio might've been at risk. That's a lot of volatility. So over time, the ETF's risk profile of these traditional maturity-based ETFs, it may move away from what the investor intended. So, to counteract this, you have to frequently rebalance your portfolio to restore your original target interest rate exposure, and that may mean you have to sell more ETFs. So if you're not paying attention, you may have had more embedded risk than you were thinking.

DS: Yeah, so well, let's double down into this fund, right? Because you just mentioned the monthly rebalance. So how does BondBloxx and their approach to the methodology of the monthly rebalancing of a fund like this, how does that compare against all the other treasury ETFs on the market? I mean, there's quite a few out there.

JG: Yeah, so we were the first at BondBloxx to market an ETF that's specifically built to address duration drift and reinvestment risk. So it offered -- and we offer eight different target duration options from six months all the way to 20 years. And unlike traditional maturity-based ETFs, the BondBloxx US Treasury ETF suite tracks the Bloomberg target duration indices and the funds -- such that the funds are only holding treasury securities that are selected and weighted to maintain a consistent interest rate target. So it's kind of like to say like these funds are really easy because they do what they say on the label. So if it's six months or one year or two years, you're getting six months duration, one year duration constantly.

So what happens is, they're systematically – the portfolios are systematically rebalanced each month. And this ensures the important aspects we're trying to deliver that duration stays on target. The portfolio continuously reflects current market yields and reinvestment risk is reduced by spreading reinvestment across monthly intervals rather than one large maturity event. So if you're reinvesting and you're resetting that target interest rate and duration on a monthly basis, you're not sort of heading down into really big rebalancing events when certain maturities come up in your portfolio and that could be a large percentage of your portfolio. The benefits of investors is really straightforward. They get precision of a custom built ladder without the burden of a constant monitoring and trading.

DS: It sounds like some nice relief right there. So I wanted to ask you, obviously, you've been talking about interest rates and how these funds kind of -- people thinking about the treasury market and we see what's going on with the administration and the swings of interest rates that we have seen over the recent years are not traditionally normal, right? But now you have an administration calling for a pretty significant cut and you look at the Fed futures and some are saying, well, maybe we'll get there, others don't. And then you look at the Fed board, some say we'll get there, some obviously don't. So how should investors be thinking about this fund if they're saying, well, do I need to be worried about a potential big interest rate swing again in the future? How should investors think about the fund in that scenario?

JG: Yeah, I think given all the uncertainty and maybe this is the time when interest rates do start coming down, instead of going up, they're going to start coming down. So they went up, they stabilized for a good chunk of time and now there's a sense that they may be coming down. Every different version of this uncertainty, we've seen investors really stay in the short end, because it reduces all of that uncertainty on the long end, which can be quite volatile. So we do recommend XHLF a lot. That's the six months target duration fund that we have.

And I think it's specific to certain scenarios that investors have that are really important right now. One is designed for investors that are seeking ultra short term. And that seems to be a really a good comfort space for people. It generates income. Interest rates are still high -- historical highs. So they can use this to generate income, manage their cash positions and maintain liquidity. So being on that short end seems to give them a little bit of coverage of the uncertainty. It keeps them generating income. And then, it's a place of liquidity for them, where they can come in and out of that space, and not making any long-term commitments into the risk of their portfolio.

So it's particularly relevant in a higher for longer interest rate environments, where short-term yields remain attractive, volatility management is essential. Even if you think rates are coming down, there's still a lot of uncertainty of how fast and how much. So, for this period of time, it seems to be a smarter place if you're concerned about uncertainty and volatility. Again, it offers a low volatility, low credit risk option because the bonds are backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. government.

DS: Now, I've got to ask you though, how is it that, I mean, 3 basis points for the expenses. How is it that you're able to keep that cost savings for the end user, for the end investor in a fund like this? I mean, obviously there's got to be, I know you said it's systematic, but 3 basis points, that's like nothing.

JG: Yeah. I mean, one of the things that we observed at BondBloxx, in addition to that duration drift problem of the existing treasury suite was also -- it wasn't priced for -- it wasn't updated in price as well. So the XLS – XLF’s -- sorry XHLF expense ratio is low. The average expense ratio for the US Treasury space in ETFs is around 14 basis points. And that compares to the average expense ratio of 6 basis points for the U.S. Treasury ETFs and BondBloxx offer. So I think that the reason we're able to offer that is first and foremost it's the right price for what it does. It's the right price for what people should be using it for in their portfolio. We want investors to keep more yield that they're getting out of this historically high interest rate environment. And also the ETF structure itself promotes operational efficiency and cost transparency. So we're just really porting through the institutional cost of running this portfolio to investors directly. We think that's the right thing to do and the right thing to do for people's portfolios.

Monthly rebalances are handled internally. It reduces the need for investors to bear additional trading or monitoring costs. So as I mentioned, if you're trying to target a certain risk parameter or duration in your portfolio, you have to constantly monitor your portfolio and then be trade in and out of ETFs to make it match. So doing this in one stop shop at a 3 basis point expense ratio is a really powerful tool for investors. And we think it's the right place for that exposure to be on average for our suite 6 basis points, the six month XHLF is 3, it's a really great tool.

DS: We love that. Thank you so much. So XHLF, let's talk about the focus of the UST Bill zero coupon securities and why this might be a fund that, a lot of people think about money market funds. Like, if I need to just do the cash position, I'll just put in money market funds. Why might a fund like this one actually kind of benefit the investor as well when they're thinking about it in that frame?

JG: Yeah. I mean, it's simple. We went over a few of these points, but it provides exposure to US Treasury Securities. It provides a consistent six month duration, which is important for risk management, so you understand exactly what the price volatility of your treasury exposure is going to do. You have an expectation. It delivers competitive yields and with minimal risk. So it's not a money market fund. We never say, what it is, but XHLF can serve a similar role in your portfolio and it often has greater yield potential. The structure of the ETF offers tax efficiency, especially to taxable investors and as with other ETFs, it delivers daily transparency, intraday liquidity, an efficient portfolio, it's an efficient tool for your portfolio and how you can integrate it into it. And then, certainly all the other things that we think about in real-time pricing and trading flexibility versus money market funds. So that's a pretty powerful liquidity tool if you ever need it, if and when.

DS: All right. So I'd like to ask you, you're deep in the treasury space with all these ETFs and have been around for so long. So I want to ask you right here, right now, what is next for the US Treasury ETF space?

JG: Well, I think what's next for the U.S. Treasury ETF space is a lot of volatility, a lot of market information. We have things that coming up in the administration that are -- people are always looking at what's going on in interest rates. So having a suite of eight treasury products that are duration targeted and specific, I think is an amazing space for investors to move around in response to what's going on in the markets. You're getting a precise low cost way to invest in XHLF in the ultra-short side of that. And like I said, that's a powerful tool as you're trying to navigate uncertainty and you want to stay in the short end, enjoy the income that it's generating, use something for your liquidity and your strategic cash. And then as you get -- as investors get more confident about what's going to be happening towards the long end, they're able to precisely move out. Maybe they can take on a two year exposure or a three year exposure. You don't have to fully go out to the full long end or right away. So I think just having the eight ETFs that have each of the years of duration and being able to move amongst them as you want is probably one of the most powerful tools you may have this year and going forward.

DS: Yeah, well said. So one ETF, you already know exactly where to go. Go look up the next duration of BondBloxx ETFs. You put that one in your portfolio and you go out to the five year and then the seven year and the 10 year. It sounds like you have everything already set up there, that ladder approach ....

JG: Yeah.

DS: So Joanna Gallegos, Co-Founder of BondBloxx, everybody, thank you so much for taking the time today to walk us through the XHLF here, U.S. six month treasury fund and why it might be right for investors' portfolios. Everybody go follow the symbol here on Seeking Alpha, add it to your portfolio, add it to your watch list, and that'll wrap it up for this week's show. We'll see you again here next week. Take care.

Follow XHLF on Seeking Alpha!

data.title: Nvidia: Poised To Break $200 After Q2 Earnings
data.description:
Introduction

My previous optimistic Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) quarterly earnings preview was good because the stock is currently 33% more expensive that it was on May 23. The Q2 release is getting closer (August 27th), and I remain optimistic before it as well. Reasons for bullishness include accelerating AI tailwinds as well as Nvidia's dominance in the industry. Moreover, Nvidia is a real earnings surprise champion that very rarely misses consensus forecasts. The pullback of the last few days looks like a prime opportunity to buy NVDA before earnings. NVDA is still a Strong Buy.

NVDA Q2 earnings preview

The company is expected to publish its Q2 earnings on August 27th, during post-market hours. Nvidia's status of the largest company in the U.S. (by market cap) means that their earnings release will be important for the entire stock market. Analysts from Wedbush are confident that Nvidia is set to deliver another blowout earnings that will make happy the broader market as well.

From my perspective, the fact that Nvidia never missed consensus forecasts over the last ten quarters is obviously good news for the broader market. Moreover, Nvidia's EPS beats are usually quite wide.

Wall Street analysts expect Nvidia to deliver a 53% year-over-year revenue growth in Q2. Forecast for the company's EPS is also positive with the expected 48% year-over-year increase for the bottom line. On the other hand, the Wall Street sentiment around Nvidia's upcoming earnings release is slightly more bearish since down revisions prevailed over upgrades over the last 90 days. This is a two-edged sword though, because beating conservative forecasts will be easier for NVDA.

The company's Data Center segment accounts for around 90% of its total sales, being still the primary engine for growth. I am highly confident in this segment because it has massive AI tailwinds behind its back. The Q2 earnings season of cloud giants (Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet/Google (GOOGL)) revealed that they remain extremely aggressive in their plans to expand their data center infrastructures.

For example, Google increased its FY2025 AI capex guidance quite aggressively, from $75 billion to $85 billion. According to recent information from TechRadar, Amazon's annual capital expenditure for data centers already topped staggering $100 billion. Microsoft is also quite aggressive, as the company announced expansion of its AI infrastructure to the heart of Europe, Austria, in late July. With all these firmly bullish developments around Nvidia, I expect an upbeat FY2026 guidance from the management as well.

Another positive factor that is likely to boost Nvidia's FY2026 guidance upgrade potential is much more certainty regarding the ability to export chips to China. I do not think that a 15% charge that will be paid from chip sales to China will be a big problem for Nvidia, taking into account its massive pricing power due to unmatched performance of its products. Therefore, I am confident that the effect of this 15% charge will be mostly passed on to Chinese customers of Nvidia and will not put pressure on margins demonstrated at the moment.

However, Nvidia's earnings are never solely about numbers and financials. CEO Jensen Huang is an exceptional visionary, and what he says about the potential of AI is highly likely to be another source of optimism for investors. Nvidia has been historically the company that opens new markets and opportunities from scratch thanks to its unparalleled commitment to innovate. If we look at the company's R&D spending line chart, the innovation budget is soaring exponentially. With such an aggressive reinvestment in innovation, I am sure that the management will unveil plans about new groundbreaking products and technologies that will further fortify Nvidia's AI ecosystem.

With its market cap of above $4 trillion and just $10 billion in debt, Nvidia has almost unlimited potential to exercise financial flexibility. Raising more funds either through debt or equity looks like no problem for Nvidia. This is another crucial factor that is highly likely to keep the management confident in their ability to ramp up R&D or M&A extremely aggressively. With Nvidia's absolutely phenomenal ROIC of around 100%, I am extremely confident that the vast majority of ventures and growth initiatives will be valuable for investors.

Therefore, I think that everything speaks bullish before Nvidia's upcoming earnings release. It is no surprise why did several prominent banks raised their price targets ahead of earnings.

Moreover, the market does not seem very greedy about Nvidia ahead of earnings, which is also good for long investors. The 14-day RSI of around 50 indicates neutral sentiment, which means that there is not much pressure from potentially unrealistic expectations.

Valuation analysis

Nvidia's estimated forward P/E ratio trajectory until FY2029 looks extremely bullish. The company's P/E is expected to compress significantly by FY2029, even considering the expected aggressive EPS growth deceleration. For the dominant force in the AI revolution this forward P/E ratio setup is no doubt bullish, signaling significant undervaluation.

The estimated FY2026 forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 40 is significantly lower than Nvidia's historical average of almost 47. The average opinion of 47 Wall Street analysts says that this fiscal year's (FY2026) EPS will be $4.35.

Strong AI tailwinds and Nvidia's dominance in processors for data centers provide Wall Street analysts with great visibility into the company's future performance, meaning that the $4.35 EPS forecast is highly reliable.

If we multiply the projected annual EPS of $4.35 by historical forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 46.88, we get the fair share price of approximately $204. With that being said, NVDA is undervalued by 17%, which is a compelling discount for the world's largest AI beneficiary.

Mitigating factors

Even for the world's largest company and absolute stock market superstar like Nvidia, there is no guarantee that they will deliver blowout earnings. Despite AI tailwinds being strong and will highly likely support massive Data Center revenue growth, there might be some temporary setbacks. For example, some temporary supply chain issues might be the reason for Q2 revenue performance below expectations.

Lower-than-expected revenue in case of temporary setbacks will not be a big fundamental problem, but the stock market is a voting machine over short term periods. Therefore, the share price might suffer in this case. Moreover, Wall Street analysts see quite limited upside potential with an average target price of $191.5. A relatively modest upside suggested by Wall Street might be another headwind for the share price after earnings.

Conclusion

I am extremely optimistic about the upcoming earnings release by Nvidia. The company has a perfect earnings surprise record over more than two years, which means that the management is highly committed to delivering on their financial plans and promises. There is no hype around the stock before Q2 earnings and the valuation looks extremely attractive, meaning that NVDA is still a no-brainer Strong Buy.
